results.question: We lost the track in the Blue Mountains and the group is getting tired. What should we do now?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

We should stop and check the map and GPS to ensure we are on the right track. If the track is lost, we should mark our last known point and check the map and GPS again. If we are unsure about the direction or terrain, we should shelter and signal for help. We should also conserve our battery and avoid pushing deeper into uncertain terrain. If we are tired, we should stop and rest. We should also use exact location information when contacting help. We should also stop and regroup at junctions and never let someone drift off alone when tired. We should also use exact location information

Lost-track field anchor: stop, keep the group together, conserve battery, make yourself visible, and signal for help.

results.question: What should I pack and check before walking to Kosciuszko in cold windy weather?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Yes, I should pack a warm and waterproof layer, navigation, and a conservative turnaround time. I should also check the official closures, forecast, fire danger and local signs before committing to a route. I should also carry insulation, rain gear, navigation, and a conservative turnaround plan. I should also check the official closures, forecast, fire danger and local signs before committing to a route. I should also carry insulation, rain gear, navigation, and a conservative turnaround plan. I should also check the official closures, forecast, fire danger and local signs before committing to a route. I should also carry

Alpine field anchor: for Kosciuszko and cold windy country, pack warm waterproof layers, navigation, food and water, and set a clear turnaround time before weather or daylight margins close.
Weather limit: offline climate notes and cloud signs are not live forecasts. Before a trip, check BoM forecasts, fire danger, flood risk, and park closures.

sources.title: Compiled official park and Tourism Australia visitor notes

results.question: Rainforest creek is rising and the track is slippery near Dorrigo. Keep going or turn back?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Turn back or wait on safe ground. Do not continue across a rising, fast, cloudy, or flood-affected creek. Keep the group together, avoid unstable crossings, and use a safer route or wait for conditions to drop.

Floodwater field anchor: do not cross rising, fast, cloudy, or flood-affected water; wait on safe ground or turn back.
Rainforest field anchor: on slippery rainforest tracks and rising creeks, turn back early, avoid unstable crossings, watch for leeches and stinging trees, and stay on formed track.
Turnaround field anchor: decide by daylight, weather, water, group condition, navigation confidence, and the slowest person.

sources.title: Bureau of Meteorology - Floods: what you need to know
Bureau of Meteorology - Warnings and alerts

results.question: At Moonee Beach we found a sea snake or unknown marine animal on the sand. What should we do?
results.answer:
Critical caution: treat this as uncertain and potentially serious. If someone is unconscious, not breathing, collapsing, severely bleeding, or rapidly worsening, call Triple Zero (000) now.

Keep well back and do not handle it. Treat sea snakes and unknown marine animals as potentially dangerous, keep people and dogs away, and call 000 for any bite, severe sting symptoms, collapse, or breathing trouble.

Marine animal field anchor: keep back from sea snakes and unknown marine animals, do not handle them, and call 000 for any bite, sting with severe symptoms, collapse, or breathing trouble.

sources.title: Queensland Poisons Information Centre - Sea creature bites and stings
Bureau of Meteorology - Warnings and alerts
Healthdirect - bites, stings, poisoning and first aid

results.question: A thunderstorm is building near the coast and we are on an exposed headland. What is the safest move?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Yes, the safest move is to leave the headland, ridges, cliffs, beaches, lone trees, water, and metal fences early. Thunderstorms can bring lightning, damaging wind, hail, intense rain, and flash flooding, so it is best to avoid these areas. Lightning and thunderstorms can also bring severe weather, bushwalker weather alerts, and flood watches, so it is important to check official warnings before heading offline. The local source notes provide additional information on lightning and thunderstorm warnings, as well as the importance of checking official warnings before heading offline. The field disclaimer tone is calm

Thunderstorm/lightning field anchor: seek substantial shelter early; leave exposed ridges, summits, beaches, cliff edges, exposed headlands, lone trees, water, and metal fences before the storm reaches you.

sources.title: Bureau of Meteorology - Storm safety tips
Bureau of Meteorology - Warnings and alerts
Bureau of Meteorology - Cloud types and weather

results.question: Someone may have eaten a wild mushroom on a hike. What should we do?
results.answer:
Critical caution: treat this as uncertain and potentially serious. If someone is unconscious, not breathing, collapsing, severely bleeding, or rapidly worsening, call Triple Zero (000) now.

Yes, if someone has eaten a wild mushroom, they should call 13 11 26 or Triple Zero (000) if they are seriously unwell. If they are unconscious, not breathing, fitting, collapsing, or severely unwell, they should call Triple Zero (000) immediately. The danger of wild mushrooms is that they can look similar to edible species, and photo identification is not safe enough for consumption decisions. If someone has eaten a wild mushroom, they should not eat it. If someone is unwell, they should seek medical attention immediately. The

Hard rule: do not eat wild mushrooms. If anyone has eaten one, call 13 11 26, or 000 if seriously unwell.
Foraging limit: do not rely on this app for consumption approval. Use a qualified local expert.

results.question: How do we treat a suspected snake bite while offline?
results.answer:
Critical caution: treat this as uncertain and potentially serious. If someone is unconscious, not breathing, collapsing, severely bleeding, or rapidly worsening, call Triple Zero (000) now.

Yes, a suspected snake bite is a serious medical emergency. Call Triple Zero (000) and keep the person still, apply a pressure immobilisation bandage if trained and available, immobilise the limb, and do not wash, cut, suck, or tourniquet the bite. The goal is to slow venom movement and get medical help. If the bite is severe, seek medical attention immediately. The snake bite emergency flow is applicable. If the bite is not severe, treat the bite as a snake-bite emergency. Do not handle or approach for a better photo; keep distance

Snake-bite field anchor: call 000, keep the person still, apply pressure immobilisation, and do not wash the bite or try to catch the snake.

results.question: I think a redback spider bit me at camp. Should I use a pressure bandage?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

No. Do not use pressure immobilisation for a likely redback bite. Wash the area, use a cold pack for pain and swelling, and call 13 11 26 or seek medical care if pain is severe, persistent, or spreads.

Redback field anchor: redbacks can cause severe pain; wash the bite, use a cold pack, call 13 11 26 or seek medical care for severe or persistent pain, and do not use pressure immobilisation.

sources.title: Queensland Poisons Information Centre - Spider bites

results.question: What if the group is running out of water and one person wants to push on?
results.answer:
Critical caution: treat this as uncertain and potentially serious. If someone is unconscious, not breathing, collapsing, severely bleeding, or rapidly worsening, call Triple Zero (000) now.

Yes, if the group is running out of water and one person wants to push on, they should first ensure they have enough water to last until they reach a safe point. They should then move to a shaded area, rest, cool the person, and seek urgent help for any signs of heat illness or exhaustion. If the group is still running out of water, they should set a turnaround time before starting and turn around when the slowest person is tiring, water is running low, weather is building, navigation is uncertain, or daylight is closing. Staying together is usually safer than sending one

Heat/water field anchor: stop in shade, ration effort, cool the person, and turn back before water or daylight margins are spent.

sources.title: Northern Territory Government - Stay safe in the heat in parks

results.question: How should we plan for heat on a remote Flinders Ranges walk?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Yes, we should plan for heat on a remote Flinders Ranges walk. Start hydrated, carry more water than a short city walk would suggest, avoid extreme heat, rest in shade if unwell, and turn around before the group is depleted. The group turnaround decision is to set a turnaround time before starting. Turn around when the slowest person is tiring, water is running low, weather is building, navigation is uncertain, or daylight is closing. Staying together is usually safer than sending one person ahead. For Ikara-Flinders Ranges National Park, plan water, turnaround time

Heat/water field anchor: stop in shade, ration effort, cool the person, and turn back before water or daylight margins are spent.

sources.title: Northern Territory Government - Stay safe in the heat in parks
Compiled official park and Tourism Australia visitor notes

results.question: A crossing in Kakadu is flooded and there may be crocodiles. What should we do?
results.answer:
Critical caution: treat this as uncertain and potentially serious. If someone is unconscious, not breathing, collapsing, severely bleeding, or rapidly worsening, call Triple Zero (000) now.

Turn back or wait on safe ground. Do not continue across a rising, fast, cloudy, or flood-affected creek. Keep the group together, avoid unstable crossings, and use a safer route or wait for conditions to drop.

Floodwater field anchor: do not cross rising, fast, cloudy, or flood-affected water; wait on safe ground or turn back.
Crocodile field anchor: obey crocodile warning signs, keep well back from water edges, and turn back from flooded crossings or places where crocodiles may be present.

results.question: We have no phone reception. What should have been done before leaving and what now?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Before leaving: cache maps and weather, share the route and return time, carry a charged phone, power bank, trip intentions, and a PLB or satellite messenger. Now: keep the group together, conserve battery, use the phone for GPS/Emergency+ if signal appears, and activate the PLB or call 000 if there is serious injury, exposure, or no safe way out.

No-reception field anchor: carry offline maps, a charged phone/power bank, trip intentions, and a PLB or satellite messenger for remote routes.
Pre-walk field anchor: check weather, route, water, daylight, closures, phone battery, offline maps, trip intentions, and a PLB before heading out of coverage.

sources.title: NSW National Parks - Personal Locator Beacons and GPS
NSW National Parks - Personal Locator Beacons and GPS
NSW National Parks - Trip intention form

results.question: The cliffs are windy and someone wants a photo near the edge. What should I say?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Say no and step back from the edge. In wind, keep everyone behind barriers or well back from cliff edges, stay together, and use a safer viewpoint for photos.

Cliff-edge field anchor: say no to photos near the edge; step back from cliff edges in wind, stay behind barriers, and use a safer viewpoint.

sources.title: Bureau of Meteorology - Warnings and alerts

results.question: How do I decide when to turn around on a day walk?
results.answer:
High caution: use this as field support, not certainty. Slow down, check the scene, and escalate early if symptoms or conditions worsen.

Yes, I can help you decide when to turn around on a day walk. First, set a turnaround time before starting. Then, consider the following factors when deciding to turn around: 1. The slowest person is tiring: If the slowest person is tiring, it's time to turn around. This is especially true when the weather is building or the water is running low. 2. Water is running low: If water is running low, it's time to turn around. This is especially true when the weather is building or the water is running low. 3. Weather is building

Turnaround field anchor: decide by daylight, weather, water, group condition, navigation confidence, and the slowest person.
